preview:

This playbook describes the priority scoring algorithm and enrichment pipeline used by platform on-call operators to triage, classify, and act on cross-service degradation alerts. It covers the data inputs to the scorer, the threshold matrix for severity buckets, recommended operator actions for each bucket...
text:
Summary:
This playbook describes the priority scoring algorithm and enrichment pipeline used by platform on-call operators to triage, classify, and act on cross-service degradation alerts. It covers the data inputs to the scorer, the threshold matrix for severity buckets, recommended operator actions for each bucket, dashboard queries, and automated remediation policies.

Intended audience:
- Platform operators
- Observability engineers
- SREs on-call for inference-router, batcher, and cache services
- Product owners responsible for SLOs

Goals and scope:
- Make alert severity predictable and repeatable across regions and tenants.
- Surface business impact (cost, affected customers, token loss) alongside technical signals.
- Provide deterministic operator steps for each priority to reduce cognitive load.
- Apply only to alerts that originate in the platform telemetry pipeline: inferen...

title: On‑call Transition Cookbook and Blameless Postmortem Cadence
preview:
Overview
========

Purpose: This page codifies a repeatable, SLO-driven approach for on‑call transitions, escalation ladders, and a blameless postmortem cadence. It is designed for engineers, SREs, and on‑call leads who perform handoffs, run game days, and own incident learning loops.

Scope: applies to Redwood Infe...
text:
Overview
========

Purpose: This page codifies a repeatable, SLO-driven approach for on‑call transitions, escalation ladders, and a blameless postmortem cadence. It is designed for engineers, SREs, and on‑call leads who perform handoffs, run game days, and own incident learning loops.

Scope: applies to Redwood Inference production services (hosted API, dedicated pools, private deployments) and covers: rotation transitions, short‑form handoff artifact, escalation timers, evidence collection, postmortem schedule, and customer status / status page templates.

Principles
----------
- SLO first: incidents are triaged against customer‑facing SLOs (availability and latency) and classification is driven by SLO impact.
- Time‑boxed actions: fast containment is prioritized; long investigations are deferred to postmortem.
- Psychological safety: no individual blame; focus on systemic causes and...

title: Postmortem: EU API latency and elevated 5xx (edge routing cache)
preview:
h1. Summary
On 2026-01-19 we experienced elevated p95 latency and intermittent HTTP 5xx for a subset of EU-hosted API traffic. The incident lasted 47 minutes end-to-end. Customer impact was concentrated in the eu-west region and primarily affected chat completions with streaming enabled.

*Customer impact:* Yes (deg...
text:
h1. Summary
On 2026-01-19 we experienced elevated p95 latency and intermittent HTTP 5xx for a subset of EU-hosted API traffic. The incident lasted 47 minutes end-to-end. Customer impact was concentrated in the eu-west region and primarily affected chat completions with streaming enabled.

*Customer impact:* Yes (degraded performance + partial errors)
*Severity:* SEV-2
*Owning engineering team:* Platform Engineering (Traffic & Edge)
*Primary services:* edge-router, request-gateway, redis-edge-cache

h1. Impact
* ~12% of EU requests exceeded the latency SLO (p95 > 3.5s)
* ~1.8% request failure rate (HTTP 502/503) for EU traffic
* No data loss; retries succeeded after mitigation

h1. Timeline (UTC)
* 10:12 - Alert fired: eu-west request-gateway 5xx > 1%
* 10:15 - On-call acknowledged; triage indicates edge-router cache churn
* 10:21 - Mitigation 1: disabled new cache key format via featu...

# Summary
We reduced the main integration risk by landing a low-overhead decision-event emission path and validating its performance impact.

# Status
- Overall: On Track
- RAG: Green

# Progress this week
- Runtime router: implemented asynchronous decision event emission behind a gate (orion_decision_events_enabled).
- Perf: measured overhead at p95 < 0.3ms for chat workloads with 1% sampling.
- Control plane: implemented a basic policy evaluation endpoint returning canonical fallback_reason values.

# Risks / blockers
- Console policy editor still missing validation rules for incompatible model variants.

# Next week
- Add compatibility validation in console (block save if variant is not compatible).
- Start end-to-end integration test: console -> control plane -> router -> decision event viewer.

# Owners
- PM: Ava Chen
- Eng: Connor O'Brien (runtime), Alex Martinez (control plane)...

text:
# Summary
Primary risk remains the runtime routing integration. We also uncovered a requirement gap around auditability of fallback decisions for enterprise customers.

# Status
- Overall: At Risk
- RAG: Red

# Progress this week
- Completed design review: agreed to keep router decisioning local for latency, but emit standardized decision events.
- Drafted event schema for policy evaluation and fallback decision logs.

# Risks / blockers
- Runtime team concern: additional per-request overhead for emitting decision events.
- Enterprise requirement: Dedicated customers want an auditable trail of why fallback occurred (capacity vs policy vs compatibility).

# Mitigations
- Emit decision events asynchronously and sample at 1% by default; allow customer-tier overrides.
- Add a "fallback_reason" enum and store only minimal fields required for audit logging.

# Next week
- Implement event em...

title: GPU graded-degradation and tenant fallback playbook
preview:
Summary:

This playbook documents the decision matrix and operational run steps to perform graded degradation and tenant-level fallbacks for GPU-backed inference pools. Graded degradation is an automated + manual strategy to keep the service available with reduced feature set and preserved SLOs when the GPU fleet is...
text:
Summary:

This playbook documents the decision matrix and operational run steps to perform graded degradation and tenant-level fallbacks for GPU-backed inference pools. Graded degradation is an automated + manual strategy to keep the service available with reduced feature set and preserved SLOs when the GPU fleet is under stress (high utilization, preemptions, network egress pressure, or supply loss). The goals are: preserve P95 latency for critical tenants, minimize cost spikes, and avoid hard failures or data loss.

Scope and applicability:
- Applies to inference GPU pools (hosted and dedicated) managed by the pool allocator service (allocator-svc).
- Targets tenant-facing request paths: predict/generate/chat/embedding.
- Not applicable to offline batch training or long-running checkpoint jobs. Those jobs should use the separate batch-queue runbook.

Owners and contacts:
- Primary r...

title: GPU service mesh patterns for inference and IaC adapter
preview:
Summary
-------
This document describes a repeatable pattern — the GPU inference service mesh — that standardizes networking, observability, and security between front-end request routers and GPU worker pools. The goal is to provide tenant-aware routing, resilient egress controls, per-tenant rate shaping, and rich t...
text:
Summary
-------
This document describes a repeatable pattern — the GPU inference service mesh — that standardizes networking, observability, and security between front-end request routers and GPU worker pools. The goal is to provide tenant-aware routing, resilient egress controls, per-tenant rate shaping, and rich telemetry with minimal application changes. It includes recommended Terraform/IaC adapter interfaces, CI/CD canary steps, and an operational runbook for mesh degradation scenarios.

Motivation
----------
- We operate heterogeneous GPU pools (on-demand, reserved, spot) across accounts and regions. Traffic must be routed by policy (latency, cost, isolation) while preserving security controls.
- Teams repeatedly reimplement sidecar, mTLS, and egress controls across projects. A standardized mesh pattern reduces duplication and improves incident response.
- Observability needs (p...

title: cross-stack-resilience-architectural-patterns-and-training-path
preview:
Overview:
This page defines a cross-stack failure-mode taxonomy, prescriptive architectural patterns to contain or degrade safely, and a concrete operator training pathway. It is intended for platform engineers, oncall operators, SREs, and platform product owners responsible for inference availability and cost-sensi...
text:
Overview:
This page defines a cross-stack failure-mode taxonomy, prescriptive architectural patterns to contain or degrade safely, and a concrete operator training pathway. It is intended for platform engineers, oncall operators, SREs, and platform product owners responsible for inference availability and cost-sensitive SLOs.

Purpose:
- Establish a common language for multi-layer failures (network, infra, runtime, model, data) so responders can triage quickly.
- Provide tested architectural mitigations and dashboard/alert specs that reduce mean time to repair (MTTR) and customer impact.
- Outline a progressive training program and gamedays to raise operator readiness.

Scope:
- Applies to Redwood-hosted runtime stacks (serving runtime, model runtime-proxy, KV cache, batcher, traffic router).
- Also covers interactions with Dedicated and Private control planes (where behavior differs)...

title: Guide: configure customer-managed audit log export (Private deployments)
preview:
# Summary
This guide explains how to enable **customer-managed audit log export** for **Redwood Private** deployments (VPC and on-prem). In Private mode, the customer typically operates the cluster and networking, and Redwood provides the export connector as part of the platform bundle.

# Preconditions

text:
# Summary
This guide explains how to enable **customer-managed audit log export** for **Redwood Private** deployments (VPC and on-prem). In Private mode, the customer typically operates the cluster and networking, and Redwood provides the export connector as part of the platform bundle.

# Preconditions
- Customer has deployed Redwood Private version that includes `audit-export-connector` (Private bundle v2.8.0+).
- Customer has created a destination bucket/container and configured encryption and retention.
- Security controls are reviewed and accepted by the customer per contract/security questionnaire.

# Supported sinks
- AWS S3
- Google Cloud Storage
- Azure Blob Storage

# Required inputs
Collect the following before changes:
- `sink`: s3|gcs|azure
- `region`: destination region
- `destination`: bucket/container name
- `prefix`: path prefix for objects
- `auth`: workload identity...

title: End-to-End SLI Composition and Cross-Service Correlation Playbook
preview:
Summary:
This playbook defines how Redwood composes end-to-end Service Level Indicators (SLIs) for multi-stage inference pipelines and how teams should correlate SLIs with distributed traces and logs to detect regressions and root cause performance issues quickly. The guidance targets hosted, dedicated, and private ...
text:
Summary:
This playbook defines how Redwood composes end-to-end Service Level Indicators (SLIs) for multi-stage inference pipelines and how teams should correlate SLIs with distributed traces and logs to detect regressions and root cause performance issues quickly. The guidance targets hosted, dedicated, and private deployments where requests traverse the API gateway, routing layer, serving runtime, and optional post-processing services.

Goals:
- Provide unambiguous SLI definitions suitable for alerting and SLA reporting.
- Ensure SLIs are computable from existing telemetry within cost and retention constraints.
- Standardize correlation keys and span/metric tags so incidents can be traced end-to-end.
- Offer pragmatic aggregation, bucketing, and sampling rules to balance observability and ingestion cost.

Scope and non-goals:
- Scope: API request paths that produce or route tokens (g...

# Summary
Orion is progressing, but we have an emerging integration risk with the runtime router path for fallback decisions.

# Status
- Overall: At Risk
- RAG: Red

# Progress this week
- Policy engine skeleton merged behind a feature flag (orion_policy_engine_enabled).
- Defined fallback precedence rules for model variants (pinning > compatibility > cost).

# Risks / blockers
- Runtime router currently applies fallback in a different code path than the control plane expects; we may need a refactor or a shim layer.
- Timeline risk: if refactor is required, console beta slips by 1–2 weeks.

# Mitigations
- Schedule a 90-minute design review with runtime + platform owners.
- Implement a temporary adapter that maps router decisions into Orion policy evaluation events.

# Next week
- Prototype adapter in staging and validate event schema.

# Owners
- PM: Ava Chen
- Eng: Alex Martinez (p...

text:
# Summary
Project Orion is the cross-team effort to unify rollout controls (canary, A/B, and automatic fallback) into a single policy surface in the Redwood Console and routing layer.

# Status
- Overall: On Track
- RAG: Green

# Progress this week
- Landed initial API contract for policy evaluation (route selector -> policy engine).
- Console wireframes approved for the policy editor v1.
- Defined success criteria for v0 (single-model canary + fallback).

# Risks / blockers
- None material.

# Decisions
- Orion v0 will support canary by percentage and automatic rollback on 5xx rate regression.

# Next week
- Implement policy engine skeleton in platform-control-plane.
- Start integration with runtime router (read-only evaluation).

# Owners
- PM: Ava Chen
- Eng: Alex Martinez (platform), Maya Srinivasan (console)

h1. Purpose
Provide consistent external communications during outages and failovers. These templates are written to be accurate even when details are still emerging.

h1. Principles
- Acknowledge impact and symptoms (errors/latency).
- Avoid speculation about root cause.
- State what we are doing next (investigating, mitigating, failing over).
- Provide the next update time.

*Body:* We are investigating elevated error rates and latency affecting a subset of requests to the Redwood Hosted API. We have identified a regional service degradation and are working to mitigate. Next update in 30 minutes.

title: Operator Safety Net: Simulation & Training Playbook
preview:
Summary:

This playbook establishes a reproducible simulation framework and an operator training curriculum for proactive detection, mitigation, and escalation during multi-signal platform degradations (cache churn, batch spikes, GPU contention, and model fallback churn). The goal is to give operators a deterministi...
text:
Summary:

This playbook establishes a reproducible simulation framework and an operator training curriculum for proactive detection, mitigation, and escalation during multi-signal platform degradations (cache churn, batch spikes, GPU contention, and model fallback churn). The goal is to give operators a deterministic, hands-on safety net for exercising runbooks before incidents occur and to validate dashboard alerting/automations end-to-end.

Why this exists:

- Many incidents are caused by subtle multi-signal interactions that are hard to reproduce in production.
- Operators need repeatable, time-boxed exercises so oncall fatigue and cognitive load can be reduced.
- Verifying dashboards, runbooks, and automation together prevents false confidence in isolated tests.

Scope and non-goals:

- In scope: platform-level degradations that affect inference latency and token-cost SLOs for mul...

title: Fidelity budgeting and runtime cost governor manual
preview:
Overview
========
This document defines the Fidelity Budgeting model and the Runtime Cost Governor (RCG) — a declarative control plane that enforces per-tenant/route fidelity budgets at serving time. The goal of the RCG is to keep customer-visible SLOs within targets while bounding per-token and per-request spend ac...
text:
Overview
========
This document defines the Fidelity Budgeting model and the Runtime Cost Governor (RCG) — a declarative control plane that enforces per-tenant/route fidelity budgets at serving time. The goal of the RCG is to keep customer-visible SLOs within targets while bounding per-token and per-request spend across mixed workloads.

Intended audience
- Runtime and scheduler engineers
- Product owners for Dedicated/Private customers
- SRE and capacity-planning teams
- Benchmark and profiling engineers

Why this exists
- Inference cost is variable by model variant, sequence length, batching shape and kernel choices.
- We need a single, auditable mechanism to trade fidelity for cost under pressure (autoscale, thermal events, capacity constraints).
- RCG ties scheduling signals, kernel hints, and quantization/variant routing decisions to a budget and provides a deterministic downgrad...

Key goals

title: INC-2025-0522 Postmortem: Private deployment capacity drop from GPU fragmentation
preview:
# Summary
On 2025-05-22, a Private (VPC) deployment experienced a 35–45% effective throughput drop and periodic timeouts. The fleet had enough raw GPUs, but fragmentation and placement constraints reduced usable capacity for the target model group.

**Incident ID:** INC-2025-0522
**Customer impact:** Single private ...
text:
# Summary
On 2025-05-22, a Private (VPC) deployment experienced a 35–45% effective throughput drop and periodic timeouts. The fleet had enough raw GPUs, but fragmentation and placement constraints reduced usable capacity for the target model group.

**Incident ID:** INC-2025-0522
**Customer impact:** Single private customer (enterprise), production
**Owning engineering team:** Eng Infra

# What happened
A model group was pinned to a specific GPU SKU and memory profile. After an unrelated maintenance rotation, the pool contained a mix of GPU SKUs and partially utilized nodes. The scheduler could not place new replicas that met the constraints, leading to queue growth and timeouts.

# Root cause
**Primary:** Fragmentation of allocatable GPU resources under strict placement constraints.
**Contributing factors:**
- Lack of a fragmentation metric and alert
- Runbook did not include defragm...

title: Third‑Party Adapter Compatibility and Safety Standard
preview:
Summary:

This standard prescribes the contract, runtime constraints, and operational hygiene for third‑party adapters (also called plugins or vendor shims) that integrate with Redwood services. The goal is to allow safe, auditable extension of runtime behavior while preserving SLOs, tenant isolation, and traceabili...
text:
Summary:

This standard prescribes the contract, runtime constraints, and operational hygiene for third‑party adapters (also called plugins or vendor shims) that integrate with Redwood services. The goal is to allow safe, auditable extension of runtime behavior while preserving SLOs, tenant isolation, and traceability.

Why this matters:
- We are exposing stable extension points (for routing, preprocessing, and postprocessing) to third parties and internal partner teams. Without clear compatibility and safety rules these adapters can cause availability incidents, data leaks, and unbounded cost.
- Adapters must be pluggable across hosted, dedicated, and private deployments with predictable behavior.

Scope:
- Applies to: adapter binaries, sidecars, WASM modules, and curated vendor integrations that run in the inference path or control plane hooks.
- Excludes: offline ETL plugins, devel...

title: Dependency Fencing and Approximation Guide
preview:
Summary
-------
This document describes an operational pattern we call dependency fencing with approximation: proactive isolation of unhealthy or high-latency downstream services combined with controlled use of approximate responses to preserve SLOs and user experience. It provides design patterns, policy examples, ...
text:
Summary
-------
This document describes an operational pattern we call dependency fencing with approximation: proactive isolation of unhealthy or high-latency downstream services combined with controlled use of approximate responses to preserve SLOs and user experience. It provides design patterns, policy examples, runbook procedures, observability/playbook templates, and ownership guidance for platform teams that operate LLM inference and platform services.

Motivation
----------
- Many Redwood platform services depend on third-party or internal systems (e.g., feature stores, rerankers, external knowledge indexes). A single slow dependency can cascade into higher tail latency and SLO breaches.
- Full failover to a simpler model (e.g., locally cached embeddings) may improve latency but change output quality. We need predictable safety mechanisms that:
 - Minimize user-visible errors
 ...

preview:

On 2025-02-11, the Hosted API experienced elevated p95 latency on /v1/chat/completions for ~23 minutes. Mitigation included activating an automatic model fallback policy to keep requests flowing while we reduced load on the primary model pool.

h2. Impact

text:
h2. Summary
On 2025-02-11, the Hosted API experienced elevated p95 latency on /v1/chat/completions for ~23 minutes. Mitigation included activating an automatic model fallback policy to keep requests flowing while we reduced load on the primary model pool.

h2. Impact
* Customer-facing: increased latency, intermittent timeouts, elevated 5xx for some bursts.
* Affected product: Redwood API (Hosted).
* Affected tiers: primarily Free and Growth tiers on the default route.

h2. Timeline (UTC)
* 17:06: Alert fired: Chat latency p95 > 4.0s (threshold 2.0s) for 5 min.
* 17:11: On-call confirmed queueing on primary model pool (rw-llama-3.1-70b-instruct).
* 17:14: Mitigation: enabled *automatic model fallback* on the router policy for the default route.
* 17:16: Router began automatically rerouting overflow traffic to rw-llama-3.1-8b-instruct (fallback target) when saturation exceeded 85%.
* 17...

text:
Summary:
This document defines a standardized deployment matrix for GPU-backed services, the Terraform/IaC workspace layout we use to manage them, and a promoted deployment policy that ties CI/CD gates, cost/egress budgets, and capacity validation into a repeatable workflow. It is intended for infra engineers, SREs, and platform owners responsible for provisioning GPU environments across dev, staging, and prod.

Goals:
- Reduce deployment drift and accidental configuration divergence between environments.
- Provide a minimal, auditable promotion path from feature branch -> staging -> production for GPU node pools, model-serving stacks, and supporting network resources.
- Enforce egress and cost guardrails before promotion.
- Make rollbacks predictable and fast for capacity-affecting changes.

Scope:
- Applies to all Terraform-managed GPU resources (node pools, autoscalers, networking,...

title: GPU CI Dependency Graph and Promote Safety Net
preview:
Summary

This document defines the GPU CI dependency graph, the artifact promotion pipeline used to move GPU images and runtime bundles from CI -> QA -> Staging -> Production, and the safety net (telemetry, automatic rollback, manual gates) we use to prevent costly regressions. It is intended for infra engineers, SR...
text:
Summary

This document defines the GPU CI dependency graph, the artifact promotion pipeline used to move GPU images and runtime bundles from CI -> QA -> Staging -> Production, and the safety net (telemetry, automatic rollback, manual gates) we use to prevent costly regressions. It is intended for infra engineers, SREs, and release engineers running GPU workloads and maintaining image/driver compatibility.

Goals

- Make promotion deterministic and auditable: every promoted artifact must show test/telemetry evidence and a signed provenance record.
- Minimize production regressions introduced by GPU runtime, kernel, or driver changes.
- Provide an automated safety net that can (a) prevent promotion, (b) enable safe canaries, and (c) rollback quickly if regressions are detected.

Scope

Applies to: GPU images, runtime bundles, kernel-module artifacts, operator charts, and IaC modules tha...

title: SDK Contract-First Local Dev Loop Playbook
preview:
Overview

This playbook documents our recommended contract-first approach to SDK integration and the local developer loop. It is intended for SDK authors, platform integrators, and application engineers who want a reproducible way to:

- bootstrap a new SDK integration using a machine-readable contract,
- iterate ra...
text:
Overview

This playbook documents our recommended contract-first approach to SDK integration and the local developer loop. It is intended for SDK authors, platform integrators, and application engineers who want a reproducible way to:

- bootstrap a new SDK integration using a machine-readable contract,
- iterate rapidly in a local environment that mirrors Redwood hosted behavior,
- ship reliable instrumentation and tests that catch API drift early, and
- provide canonical fixes for common developer support issues.

Why contract-first?

Working from a contract (OpenAPI/JSON Schema/IDLs) reduces misunderstanding between runtime, client SDKs, and product teams. It enables automated generation of request/response shapes, validation, test scaffolding, and a local mock server that can be shipped as part of the SDK developer experience.

Audience and scope

- Audience: SDK maintainers, inte...

title: Control plane credential dependencies (inventory)
preview:
# Purpose
This page lists known **credential dependencies** for Redwood control plane and core production services. Use it to ensure secret rotation is complete (i.e., every consumer is updated).

# How to use this inventory

text:
# Purpose
This page lists known **credential dependencies** for Redwood control plane and core production services. Use it to ensure secret rotation is complete (i.e., every consumer is updated).

# How to use this inventory
- For a given secret, identify all services that read it.
- Confirm whether the service reloads secrets dynamically or requires a restart.
- Confirm whether the secret supports a dual-valid overlap window.

# Inventory table
Format: Service | Secret name/path | Used for | Reload behavior | Notes

- api-gateway | vault:prod/api-gateway/jwt-signing-key | signing outbound session tokens | requires deploy | overlap supported via key ring
- router | vault:prod/router/service-token | service-to-service auth | hot reload every 5 min | retry storms possible if token invalid
- billing-meter | vault:prod/billing/warehouse-creds | analytics warehouse access | requires restar...

preview:

text:
# Purpose
This runbook describes how to manually shift (fail over) production inference traffic for the Hosted API between regions or clusters during GPU capacity shortages, elevated error rates, or regional degradation.

# Scope
- Applies to: chat + embeddings endpoints served by Redwood API (Hosted).
- Not for: Redwood Dedicated customer-specific pools (see dedicated incident playbooks).

# Triggers (choose one)
- Sustained 5xx > 1% for 10+ minutes in a region
- p95 latency > 2x baseline for 15+ minutes with correlated GPU saturation
- GPU capacity deficit (queue depth increasing; admission control engaged)
- Cloud provider incident impacting a region

# Preconditions
- Incident declared or oncall lead approval
- Status page draft ready (if customer-visible)
- Confirm target region has headroom (GPU free capacity + router capacity)

# Definitions
- "Traffic shift": changing routing ...

title: Request termination and graceful cancellation specification
preview:
Overview

This page defines Redwood's cross-stack specification for request termination and graceful cancellation. It standardizes client and server behaviors across HTTP/1.1, HTTP/2, gRPC, and streaming transports (server-sent, websockets, and tokenized streaming used by Redwood). The goal is to reduce resource lea...
text:
Overview

This page defines Redwood's cross-stack specification for request termination and graceful cancellation. It standardizes client and server behaviors across HTTP/1.1, HTTP/2, gRPC, and streaming transports (server-sent, websockets, and tokenized streaming used by Redwood). The goal is to reduce resource leakage, avoid inconsistent model state, and enable predictable observability and billing when requests are cancelled or aborted.

Audience
- API designers and backend engineers
- Runtime and serving teams
